patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 0/3] perf record: fix multi-record Zstd compression

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series fixes a regression in perf record that causes it to abort recording when using Zstd compression, producing an undecompressable perf.data file. The patches address three issues: a 'Bad address' error, an output-buffer overrun, and a broken zstd-error fallback. The approach taken is to fix the bugs in the compressor function, specifically in how it handles multiple compressed records and buffer sizes.

review_comments.author: Namhyung Kim
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Namhyung Kim pointed out that the patch does not handle the case where the compressed data is larger than the output buffer size, which can lead to a buffer overflow.

Reviewer Namhyung Kim requested a rebase of the patch series onto the latest perf-tools-next branch, as it cannot apply anymore due to changes in that branch.
review_comments.raw_body:
Hello,

On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 07:17:00AM +0000, Dmitry Ilvokhin wrote:

---

Can you please remove the blank line here?

Also, this series cannot apply anymore.  Please rebase onto the latest
perf-tools-next.

Thanks,
Namhyung

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 3/3] perf record: Don't store raw data on zstd compression failure

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch prevents the perf record tool from storing raw data when ZSTD compression fails, which would lead to incorrect results and potential buffer overflows. Instead of copying the uncompressed input into the output buffer, it propagates the error. This fix addresses a problem introduced by a previous patch that added support for Zstd streaming-based compression.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 2/3] perf record: Avoid overrunning the zstd output buffer

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the Zstd compression API used by perf record, where the output buffer could be overrun when compressing data. The fix involves passing the remaining space in the output buffer to a new function process_comp_header(), which checks if the record header and padding would fit before writing them. If not, it returns -1 to stop compressing. This prevents the API from writing past the end of the output buffer, fixing an underflow bug introduced by a previous patch.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

version_history.subject: [PATCH v2 1/3] perf record: Fix multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records per push

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in perf record where multiple PERF_RECORD_COMPRESSED2 records are written to the file when the compressed output exceeds the maximum record size, leading to mis-framed data that cannot be decompressed. The fix involves changing how the header is set for each record, ensuring that it accurately reflects the compressed data size and aligns properly with 8-byte boundaries. This change also reduces the maximum record size by the size of a u64 to prevent overflows in the header.size field.

review_comments.author: Namhyung Kim
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer Namhyung Kim pointed out that the patch description does not clearly explain why the current fixup is wrong by construction and requested a more detailed explanation of the problem.

Reviewer Namhyung Kim requested a rebase of the patch series onto the latest perf-tools-next branch due to inapplicability and also asked for removal of an empty line in the code.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v2 0/2] syscall_user_dispatch: add build/runtime configs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 1/2] syscall_user_dispatch: Make it configurable in Kconfig
series_items.patch_summary: This patch introduces a new Kconfig option, CONFIG_SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH, to make Syscall User Dispatch configurable. The feature allows a thread's system calls to be intercepted and redirected to a userspace signal handler outside an allowed IP address range.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Expressed confusion over the acronym 'IP' in the patch description, suggesting it should be clarified or rephrased for better understanding.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Fri, Jul 03, 2026 at 09:58:58PM -0400, Gregory Price wrote:
> +config SYSCALL_USER_DISPATCH
> +	bool "Syscall User Dispatch"
> +	depends on GENERIC_ENTRY
> +	default y
> +	help
> +	  Syscall User Dispatch lets a thread have its own system calls outside
> +          an allowed IP address range to be intercepted and redirected to a
> +          userspace signal handler.

I was very confused when I read this.  IP expands to Internet Protocol
long before it gets to Instruction Pointer in my brain.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v2 2/2] syscall_user_dispatch: add kernel.syscall_user_dispatch sysctl
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new sysctl called kernel.syscall_user_dispatch to control whether userspace can arm syscall user dispatch via prctl or ptrace. The default value is 1, allowing arming of syscall user dispatch. Disabling it while a task has already armed it will not cause the task's dispatch to become inactive, but rather remain active until the user attempts to disable or re-enable it via prctl or ptrace. This prevents undefined behavior in userland when translating non-Linux syscalls to Linux syscalls.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new configurable feature, syscall_user_dispatch, which allows users to enable or disable the feature at both build and runtime. The feature is currently compiled in by default for all architectures that support CONFIG_GENERIC_SYSCALL, but this patch makes it optional.

review_comments.author: Matthew Wilcox
review_comments.summary: Expressed confusion over the patch's explanation of IP expansion, suggesting a need for clearer documentation or explanation.

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH v3 00/11] mm: PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs

version_history.subject: [PATCH v3 00/11] mm: PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 01/11] mm: add PMD swap entry detection support
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for detecting PMD (Page Map Directory) swap entries in the Linux kernel. Currently, when a huge page is swapped out, it's split into 512 PTE-level swap entries. To preserve huge page information across swap cycles, this patch introduces infrastructure to detect and handle PMD-level swap entries instead. The patch adds helper functions for architectures that support PMD softleaf entries and provides no-op fallbacks for those without such support. This is the first of 11 patches in a series aimed at improving memory management and preserving huge page information during swapping.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 02/11] mm: add PMD swap entry splitting support
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for splitting a PMD swap entry into 512 PTE swap entries in the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) subsystem. This is done by adding a new branch to the __split_huge_pmd_locked() function that handles PMD swap entries differently than migration splits. The patch preserves flags such as soft_dirty, uffd_wp, and exclusive when splitting the PMD swap entry into PTEs. The change also includes checks to prevent potential issues with uninitialized variables in the swap branch.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 03/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in fork path
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of PMD (Page Map Directory) swap entries during the fork process in Linux. It introduces a new function, `swap_dup_entries_direct`, which can duplicate a contiguous range of swap slots in one call. The patch also modifies the `copy_huge_non_present_pmd` function to 'copy' PMD swap entries instead of splitting them, preserving the Huge Page (THP). This change mirrors the behavior of `copy_nonpresent_pte` for PTEs and ensures that the PMD is stable across retries if necessary. The patch also includes modifications to handle GFP_ATOMIC table allocation failures and retries with GFP_KERNEL.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 04/11] mm: zswap: add range lookup for large-folio swapin
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a range lookup feature to the zswap compression algorithm, allowing it to handle large folios by scanning the covered range and checking if any slots are still in zswap. If no slot is found in zswap, the swap_read_folio() function reads from the backing device. The patch also introduces a new function, zswap_range_has_entry(), which enables PMD swap-entry consumers to make the same range decision before attempting PMD-order swapin.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 05/11] mm: swap in PMD swap entries as whole THPs during swapoff
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of swap entries during swapoff by introducing a new function, `swap_pmd_cache_lookup`, which classifies the swap cache behind a PMD swap entry as empty, backed by one PMD-sized folio, or requiring per-page handling. The patch also introduces a new function, `unuse_pmd`, which swaps in PMD-level swap entries as whole THPs during swapoff. If a PMD-order folio cannot be allocated, the PMD swap entry is split into PTE-level entries via `__split_huge_pmd`. This change aims to optimize swapoff performance by reducing the number of page-table lookups required.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 06/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in non-present PMD walkers
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates various Linux kernel functions to handle PMD (Page Map Directory) swap entries in non-present PMD walkers, mirroring the behavior of PTE-level equivalents. The changes include adding a new helper function for accounting swap and swap_pss, updating several functions to handle swap entries alongside migration entries, and modifying functions that handle faults, madvise operations, and memory policy enforcement. The goal is to ensure correct handling of swapped-out THPs (Transparent Huge Pages) in various kernel paths.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 07/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in MADV_WILLNEED
series_items.patch_summary: This patch enhances the handling of PMD swap entries in MADV_WILLNEED operations. It ensures that PMD-sized folios are properly prefetched and avoids unnecessary splitting of PMD swap entries. The patch introduces a new approach to handle PMD swap entries under pmd_trans_huge_lock(), which includes checking for split cache state, zswap entries, or existing PMD-sized folios. If any of these conditions are met, the patch splits the PMD swap entry and asks the walker to retry through the PTE path. Otherwise, it pins the swap device and reads the folio in at PMD order via swapin_sync(). This approach keeps the subsequent fault on the do_huge_pmd_swap_page() path and avoids order-0 readahead unnecessarily splitting the PMD swap entry.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 08/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in UFFDIO_MOVE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for moving PMD-level swap entries in the UFFDIO_MOVE operation. It introduces a new function, move_swap_pmd(), which moves the swap entry whole-PMD and re-anchors the PMD-sized swap-cache folio's anon rmap to the destination VMA. The patch also updates the dispatcher in move_pages_huge_pmd() to wait for migration on a PMD migration entry and route PMD swap entries through move_swap_pmd(). This ensures that UFFDIO_MOVE operations can handle PMD swap entries correctly, even when they are swapped out.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 09/11] mm: handle PMD swap entry faults on swap-in
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new handler for PMD swap entry faults on swap-in, allowing the kernel to handle large page mappings in one shot. It introduces a new function do_huge_pmd_swap_page() that resolves the entire 2 MB mapping by looking up the folio in the swap cache, allocating a PMD-order folio if necessary, and restoring soft_dirty and uffd_wp from the swap PMD. The handler also updates vmf->orig_pmd and calls wp_huge_pmd() to COW immediately when the resulting PMD is read-only but the fault was a write. If PMD-order resources are unavailable, it splits the PMD swap entry into 512 PTE swap entries via __split_huge_pmd().

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 10/11] mm: install PMD swap entries on swap-out
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the swap-out process for huge pages by installing PMD-level swap entries instead of splitting them into individual PTEs, reducing overhead and improving performance. It mirrors the existing PTE swap-out path at PMD granularity, handling zswap and adjusting MM_ANONPAGES / MM_SWAPENTS accordingly. The patch introduces a new function set_pmd_swap_entry() to install the swap PMD, which preserves original soft-dirty, uffd-wp, and exclusive bits. This change aims to improve the efficiency of huge page swapping and reduce the load on khugepaged.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v3 11/11] selftests/mm: add PMD swap entry tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new test suite to exercise PMD swap entry paths in the Linux kernel. The tests cover various scenarios, including swapping out and in of THPs, fork operations, write faults, munmap, mprotect, UFFDIO_MOVE, mremap, pagemap reading, mincore, MADV_FREE, MADV_WILLNEED, and swapoff. The test suite also accounts for the presence or absence of zswap, which can affect how PMD swap entries are handled. The tests verify that data correctness is maintained across these operations and that the kernel behaves as expected in different configurations.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new feature to Linux kernel called PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs (Transparent Huge Pages). When a PMD-mapped anonymous THP is swapped out, the current implementation splits it into PTE-level swap entries. This patch series changes this behavior so that a single PMD-level swap entry is created instead, allowing the huge mapping to survive the swap round-trip and be restored directly on swap-in without needing khugepaged to collapse the range later. The PMD swap entry is a compact page-table encoding for HPAGE_PMD_NR consecutive swap slots, and its handling is designed to work seamlessly with existing kernel features like zswap and khugepaged.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the PMD swap entry detection support by explaining that it currently splits PMD-mapped THPs into PTE-level swap entries, but later patches will install single PMD-level swap entries instead. The author added infrastructure to detect those entries and provided generic no-op PMD swap exclusive fallbacks for architectures without PMD softleaf support.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about the handling of PMD swap entries in __split_huge_pmd_locked() by adding a new branch that splits a PMD swap entry into 512 PTE swap entries, preserving soft_dirty, uffd_wp, and exclusive flags. The author acknowledged that page and folio are left uninitialized in this branch to avoid freezing=true, but added a VM_WARN_ON_ONCE(freeze) to catch any future caller that breaks this invariant.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about handling PMD swap entries during fork and copy_huge_pmd(). They taught copy_huge_non_present_pmd() to duplicate PMD swap entries instead of splitting them, mirroring the behavior for PTEs. The function now returns an error if it fails to allocate memory for the swap table, and retries with GFP_KERNEL allocation. This change preserves the THP during fork.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about zswap's handling of large folios, explaining that they should only be swapped in when their whole range is expected to be on disk and adding a new function zswap_range_has_entry() to check if any slot in the range is still in zswap. The author confirmed that PMD swap-entry consumers will split before calling into PMD-order swapin whenever any slot is still in zswap, ensuring safe handling of large folios.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about handling PMD swap entries during swapoff by adding unuse_pmd() and calling it from unuse_pmd_range(). This mirrors the existing unuse_pte_range(), but operates at PMD granularity. The function returns 0 on success, -EAGAIN if the swap cache folio no longer matches the entry or the PMD changed under the lock, and -EIO if the folio is not uptodate.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about handling PMD swap entries in various mm functions, specifically smaps_pmd_entry(), clear_soft_dirty_pmd(), and madvise_free_huge_pmd(). They added new helpers to account for swap entries in smaps_account_swap() and modified several functions to handle PMD swap entries correctly. The author confirmed that the changes will be included in v2 of the patch series.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about MADV_WILLNEED on PMD swap entries, explaining that swapin_walk_pmd_entry() skips non-present PMDs and thus MADV_WILLNEED is a no-op. They also described how to handle PMD swap entries under pmd_trans_huge_lock(), including pinning the swap device and reading the folio in at PMD order if necessary.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about move_pages_huge_pmd() returning -ENOENT for non-trans_huge, non-migration PMDs by adding a new function move_swap_pmd() that moves the swap entry whole-PMD and re-anchors the anon rmap. The dispatcher in move_pages_huge_pmd() now waits for migration on a PMD migration entry and routes PMD swap entries through move_swap_pmd().
sentiment_signals: added new code to address it

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: The author addressed a concern about handling PMD swap entries in the fault path, explaining that they added a new handler do_huge_pmd_swap_page() to directly map PMD-sized folios from the swap cache and split the entry into PTE-level swap entries when necessary. The author confirmed that this approach mirrors the existing PTE-path handling for swap-in.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Andrew Morton asked the maintainers to review existing code, specifically pointing out a potential issue in move_pages_huge_pmd() where a stack-local pgtable_t* is passed to pmd_migration_entry_wait(), and suggested that AI review may have found additional issues
review_comments.raw_body:
Thanks.

AI review said a lot about the new code.

It also might have found a bunch of issues in the existing code.  I
looked at one (move_pages_huge_pmd() passing a stack-local pgtable_t*
to pmd_migration_entry_wait()) and it looks legit.

So can the people who work on this code please take a look?

	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260703173903.3789516-1-usama.arif@linux.dev

patches_submitted.subject: [PATCH 0/6] mm: preparatory patches for PMD level swap entries

version_history.subject: [PATCH 0/6] mm: preparatory patches for PMD level swap entries

series_items.subject: [PATCH 1/6] mm: add softleaf_to_pmd() and convert existing callers
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series introduces a new function, softleaf_to_pmd(), which generates PMD entries from leaf entries. It also converts existing callers of swp_entry_to_pmd() to use the new function. The goal is to maintain symmetry in the softleaf abstraction and prepare for upcoming PMD swap entry support.

review_comments.author: Lance Yang
review_comments.summary: Identified the patch as generally correct and suggested adding a Reviewed-by tag to acknowledge their review. They expressed no technical concerns or suggestions.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 09:34:38AM -0700, Usama Arif wrote:
>Add softleaf_to_pmd() as the PMD counterpart to softleaf_to_pte(),
>completing the symmetry of the softleaf abstraction for page table
>leaf entries.
>
>The upcoming PMD swap entry support needs to construct PMD entries
>from swap entries. Converting existing swp_entry_to_pmd() callers
>to softleaf_to_pmd() in a prep patch keeps the feature patches
>focused on new functionality rather than mixing refactoring with
>new code.
>
>Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
>Reviewed-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
>Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
>---

LGTM. Feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>

series_items.subject: [PATCH 2/6] mm: extract mm_prepare_for_swap_entries() helper
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series extracts a helper function, mm_prepare_for_swap_entries(), to ensure the mm is added to init_mm.mmlist for swapoff to find and unuse its swap entries. The double-checked locking pattern was previously open-coded in try_to_unmap_one() and copy_nonpresent_pte().

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Commented that the function name is self-explaining and suggested removing a comment. Acknowledged the patch with Acked-by.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 3/6] fs/proc: use softleaf_has_pfn() in pagemap PMD walker
series_items.patch_summary: This patch fixes a bug in the pagemap PMD walker by adding a check to ensure that only non-present PMDs that encode a PFN are looked up using softleaf_to_page(). This prevents crashes when encountering non-present PMDs of other types, such as upcoming PMD-level swap entries. The fix is achieved by introducing a guard with softleaf_has_pfn(), matching the existing handling for non-present PTEs in pte_to_pagemap_entry().

series_items.subject: [PATCH 4/6] mm/huge_memory: move softleaf_to_folio() inside migration branch
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the call to softleaf_to_folio() inside a migration branch in the change_non_present_huge_pmd() function, ensuring that it only converts PFN entries to folio pointers when they are actually used for migration. This prevents bogus pointer creation and potential crashes on mprotect() when swap entries are present.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 5/6] mm/migrate_device: move softleaf_to_folio() inside device-private branch
series_items.patch_summary: This patch moves the call to softleaf_to_folio() inside a device-private branch in the mm/migrate_device.c file, ensuring that the folio is only converted when it's actually needed and preventing potential issues with bogus folio pointers or pfn_to_page validation.

review_comments.author: David Hildenbrand (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Identified the patch as correct and suitable for inclusion in the kernel, with no further comments or concerns.

series_items.subject: [PATCH 6/6] mm: rename ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF
series_items.patch_summary: This patch renames the CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION configuration option to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF, which more accurately reflects its purpose of enabling PMD-level softleaf machinery. The rename is a pure change and does not introduce any functional changes. The affected architectures remain unchanged, with x86, arm64, s390, riscv, loongarch, and powerpc on PPC_BOOK3S_64 still selecting this option. This change aims to improve the clarity of the configuration options.

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer David pointed out that the description of CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION as having 'grown to gate the entire PMD-level softleaf machinery' is inaccurate, specifically mentioning device-private entries which rely on migration support and are not affected by the rename.

Reviewer David (Arm) suggested using either ARCH_HAS_PMD_SOFTLEAVES or HAVE_ARCH_PMD_SOFTLEAVES instead of ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF, and agreed that the config option rename makes sense.
review_comments.raw_body:
device-private really relies on migration support.

See do_huge_pmd_device_private() where we migrate back to CPU memory.

The whole concept is pased on migration. So that's why it currently makes sense.

So "has grown to" is not quite accurate. It's a different story with swap
entries (that can exist even without migration support).

---

Hm, that makes me wonder what it would take to make this all just be implicitly
supported by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE.

Looks like someone would have the courage to touch stuff like arc, mips and
sparc64 (+arm and x86, but that's less of a concern :) ).

So yeah, renaming the config option makes sense. The config space is the wild west.

We have

	PGTABLE_HAS_HUGE_LEAVES

and stuff like

	HAVE_ARCH_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_PUD
	HAVE_ARCH_SOFT_DIRTY
	ARCH_HAS_GIGANTIC_PAGE
	ARCH_HAS_PTE_SPECIAL

We do have a limited number of "SUPPORTS" (which is longer than HAS), but I
would either use


	ARCH_HAS_PMD_SOFTLEAVES

or

	HAVE_ARCH_PMD_SOFTLEAVES


Apart from that LGTM.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Zi Yan mentioned that they previously had a no-go for s390 architecture and it's unclear if the new name ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF resolves this issue.
review_comments.raw_body:
You mean me back in 2018[1]? ;)

At least I got a no go for s390.


[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20180426142804.180152-1-zi.yan@sent.com/


Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: The reviewer notes that the original patch was submitted 8 years ago and a related change has since been merged upstream for s390, which enables THP_SWAP and THP_MIGRATION. The reviewer requests that the original patchset be revived.
sentiment_signals: request for revival of original patchset
review_comments.raw_body:
hah! and 8 years later it seems to exist upstream :)

commit 10dd5a0009898ba35eafeb6087e5c83b84742ff1

So, with that out of the way, could you revive your patchset?

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Zi Yan noted the patch's rename of CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF is incomplete and requested it be updated in all relevant Kconfig files.

Sure. Will put that on my TODO list.

Yan, Zi

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series is a preparatory step for introducing PMD level swap entries in the Linux kernel. It cleans up existing code by adding helper functions, refactoring open-coded logic, and defensive checks to preserve current behavior. The patches do not introduce new functionality but rather lay the groundwork for future changes. They are safe to merge independently and will be followed by a series that introduces PMD swap entries. The approach taken is to simplify and modularize existing code to make it easier to review and maintain.

review_comments.author: Andrew Morton
review_comments.summary: Andrew Morton noted that the patch series is not yet in its final form and suggested queuing it in mm-new for early testing, as he believes it will be upstreamed in the next cycle.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, 30 Jun 2026 09:34:37 -0700 Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:

> This is the preparatory part of the PMD page table swapin work. 

Thanks.

This comes reasonably reviewed, so I'll queue it in mm-new.

Why?

- Human review coverage and AI review make me believe this isn't the
  final version at all, but I do believe this is something we want in
  7.2, and getting it under test early will help to push it along.

- Getting it in there early means that others will base their work on
  material which will probably be upstreamed this cycle, so this gives
  them a more accurate base against which to work.

Sashiko did find a lot to talk about, some of it pre-existing so can
people who work on this code please take a look at

	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260630164143.1595669-1-usama.arif@linux.dev

---

On Wed, 1 Jul 2026 09:04:33 +0100 Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org> wrote:

> On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 12:50:24PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> > On Tue, 30 Jun 2026 09:34:37 -0700 Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:
> >
> > > This is the preparatory part of the PMD page table swapin work.
> >
> > Thanks.
> >
> > This comes reasonably reviewed, so I'll queue it in mm-new.
> >
> > Why?
> >
> > - Human review coverage and AI review make me believe this isn't the
> >   final version at all, but I do believe this is something we want in
> >   7.2, and getting it under test early will help to push it along.
> >
> > - Getting it in there early means that others will base their work on
> >   material which will probably be upstreamed this cycle, so this gives
> >   them a more accurate base against which to work.
> >
> > Sashiko did find a lot to talk about, some of it pre-existing so can
> > people who work on this code please take a look at
> >
> > 	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260630164143.1595669-1-usama.arif@linux.dev
> >
> >
> 
> Hm, my understanding was that mm-new is where we take everything submitted for
> early testing?
> 
> Did something change?

mm-new has always been a few day front-end to mm-unstable.  To protect
linux-next from brand new material.  It serves no other purpose.

Almost.  I'll very occasionally use it for short-term not-for-upstream
playthings.

> Generally anything submitted early in the cycle is expected to land in the cycle
> unless there's significant adverse review or other reasons it can't be taken.

Yup.  Don't want anything in mm.git which isn't expected to be
upstreamed in the next merge window.

Nowadays I'm reluctant to add anything which hasn't had a decent scan
from reviewers.  If it's something I'd particularly like to get
upstreamed I'll very occasionally add it early, to help push it along.

review_comments.author: Lorenzo Stoakes
review_comments.summary: Lorenzo Stoakes questioned the purpose of mm-new as a front-end to mm-unstable, citing that it only protects linux-next from brand new material and wondered if there's much point in having it given the existence of CI testing.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 12:50:24PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> On Tue, 30 Jun 2026 09:34:37 -0700 Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:
>
> > This is the preparatory part of the PMD page table swapin work.
>
> Thanks.
>
> This comes reasonably reviewed, so I'll queue it in mm-new.
>
> Why?
>
> - Human review coverage and AI review make me believe this isn't the
>   final version at all, but I do believe this is something we want in
>   7.2, and getting it under test early will help to push it along.
>
> - Getting it in there early means that others will base their work on
>   material which will probably be upstreamed this cycle, so this gives
>   them a more accurate base against which to work.
>
> Sashiko did find a lot to talk about, some of it pre-existing so can
> people who work on this code please take a look at
>
> 	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260630164143.1595669-1-usama.arif@linux.dev
>
>

Hm, my understanding was that mm-new is where we take everything submitted for
early testing?

Did something change?

Generally anything submitted early in the cycle is expected to land in the cycle
unless there's significant adverse review or other reasons it can't be taken.

Thanks, Lorenzo

---

+cc Mike re: CI.

On Wed, Jul 01, 2026 at 04:46:13PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> On Wed, 1 Jul 2026 09:04:33 +0100 Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org> wrote:
>
> > On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 12:50:24PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> > > On Tue, 30 Jun 2026 09:34:37 -0700 Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:
> > >
> > > > This is the preparatory part of the PMD page table swapin work.
> > >
> > > Thanks.
> > >
> > > This comes reasonably reviewed, so I'll queue it in mm-new.
> > >
> > > Why?
> > >
> > > - Human review coverage and AI review make me believe this isn't the
> > >   final version at all, but I do believe this is something we want in
> > >   7.2, and getting it under test early will help to push it along.
> > >
> > > - Getting it in there early means that others will base their work on
> > >   material which will probably be upstreamed this cycle, so this gives
> > >   them a more accurate base against which to work.
> > >
> > > Sashiko did find a lot to talk about, some of it pre-existing so can
> > > people who work on this code please take a look at
> > >
> > > 	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260630164143.1595669-1-usama.arif@linux.dev
> > >
> > >
> >
> > Hm, my understanding was that mm-new is where we take everything submitted for
> > early testing?
> >
> > Did something change?
>
> mm-new has always been a few day front-end to mm-unstable.  To protect
> linux-next from brand new material.  It serves no other purpose.

Well it only serves a purpose if it gives us better testing.

We have a CI running tests now though, Mike - is that predicated on material
being in mm-new or does it just grab the email?

If the CI is just grabbing email I wonder whether there's much purpose in the
mm-new branch at all.

>
> Almost.  I'll very occasionally use it for short-term not-for-upstream
> playthings.
>
> > Generally anything submitted early in the cycle is expected to land in the cycle
> > unless there's significant adverse review or other reasons it can't be taken.
>
> Yup.  Don't want anything in mm.git which isn't expected to be
> upstreamed in the next merge window.
>
> Nowadays I'm reluctant to add anything which hasn't had a decent scan
> from reviewers.  If it's something I'd particularly like to get
> upstreamed I'll very occasionally add it early, to help push it along.

Well the question is what is considered to be a decent scan from reviewers?

Is it tags on all patches? As a glance at mm-new suggests otherwise.

I guess moving forwards with mm-next we can figure out the rules specifically on
this kind of thing.

Maintainer signoff is the key indicator of something being acceptable for the
cycle.

But I think probably if we have a topic branch of stuff we're considering for
upstream (but not the stable branch which should require maintainer signoff), a
good proportion of tags (and review from everybody matters and is important)
maybe should be the metric.

I am wondering about my series(es) in general though as I'm likely to cause
some significant merge conflict pain for people if that's delayed too
much. But sure we can wait for more review coverage on that I guess.

Thanks, Lorenzo

---

+cc Mark

On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 04:08:03PM +0300, Mike Rapoport wrote:
> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 11:48:24AM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> > +cc Mike re: CI.
> >
> > On Wed, Jul 01, 2026 at 04:46:13PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> > > On Wed, 1 Jul 2026 09:04:33 +0100 Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org> wrote:
...
> > > > Hm, my understanding was that mm-new is where we take everything submitted for
> > > > early testing?
> > > >
> > > > Did something change?
> > >
> > > mm-new has always been a few day front-end to mm-unstable.  To protect
> > > linux-next from brand new material.  It serves no other purpose.
> >
> > Well it only serves a purpose if it gives us better testing.
> >
> > We have a CI running tests now though, Mike - is that predicated on material
> > being in mm-new or does it just grab the email?
>
> CI grabs patches from patchwork and tries to apply them to one of the three
> bases: mm-unstable, mm-new or the latest Linus' commit merged into mm.git.
>
> If patches apply, the other bases are not checked.
>
> This patches applied cleanly to mm-unstable and successfully passed (at
> least once :) )
> https://github.com/linux-mm/linux-mm/actions/runs/28544575927/
>
> > If the CI is just grabbing email I wonder whether there's much purpose in the
> > mm-new branch at all.
>
> I can't say that CI I run has a great coverage. I don't know what coverage
> mm-new has though

Well we're very grateful for what coverage it has :) and that's nice to have it
work with email like that also, makes life simpler.

Mark - are you running tests based on mm-new? I'm not sure where things stand on
that?

>
> > Thanks, Lorenzo
>
> --
> Sincerely yours,
> Mike.

Thanks, Lorenzo

---

On Wed, Jul 01, 2026 at 10:03:21PM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
> > CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION started life gating just PMD-level
> > migration entries, but has grown to gate the entire PMD-level softleaf
> > machinery: migration entries, device-private entries, and soon swap
> > entries.
>
> device-private really relies on migration support.
>
> See do_huge_pmd_device_private() where we migrate back to CPU memory.
>
> The whole concept is pased on migration. So that's why it currently makes sense.
>
> So "has grown to" is not quite accurate. It's a different story with swap
> entries (that can exist even without migration support).
>
>
> > Rename CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD
> > _SOFTLEAF to make this clear.  This is a pure rename: the set of
> > selecting architectures (x86, arm64, s390, riscv, loongarch, and
> > powerpc on PPC_BOOK3S_64) and the gating semantics are unchanged.
>
> Hm, that makes me wonder what it would take to make this all just be implicitly
> supported by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE.
>
> Looks like someone would have the courage to touch stuff like arc, mips and
> sparc64 (+arm and x86, but that's less of a concern :) ).
>
> So yeah, renaming the config option makes sense. The config space is the wild west.
>
> We have
>
> 	PGTABLE_HAS_HUGE_LEAVES
>
> and stuff like
>
> 	HAVE_ARCH_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_PUD
> 	HAVE_ARCH_SOFT_DIRTY
> 	ARCH_HAS_GIGANTIC_PAGE
> 	ARCH_HAS_PTE_SPECIAL
>
> We do have a limited number of "SUPPORTS" (which is longer than HAS), but I
> would either use
>
>
> 	ARCH_HAS_PMD_SOFTLEAVES
>
> or
>
> 	HAVE_ARCH_PMD_SOFTLEAVES

Yeah it'd be GREAT to simplify all this!

>
>
> Apart from that LGTM.

Can I just say I love this patch in general though? Softleaf all the things :))

>
> --
> Cheers,
>
> David

Cheers, Lorenzo

---

On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 05:57:34PM +0100, Mark Brown wrote:
> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 05:53:48PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> > On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 04:08:03PM +0300, Mike Rapoport wrote:
>
> > > I can't say that CI I run has a great coverage. I don't know what coverage
> > > mm-new has though

> > work with email like that also, makes life simpler.
>
> > Mark - are you running tests based on mm-new? I'm not sure where things stand on
> > that?
>
> I currently test -next, mainline and stable plus my own stuff.

Ack thanks!

I guess in the long run then we can figure out a way to not necessarily even
require mm-new at all, and instead let our CI try to handle as much as it can
before -> linux-next :)

As that'd be the nicest way and help reduce confusion about branches for
mm-next.

Cheers, Lorenzo

---

On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 07:10:34PM +0200, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> On 7/2/26 19:08, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary: Author acknowledged that the patch series is not yet final, but agrees it's worth merging early to provide a stable base for future work. They also confirmed they'll send the PMD core series once this one is merged in mm-new.
review_comments.raw_body:

review_comments.author: David (Arm)
review_comments.summary: Reviewer David (Arm) noted that the comment about CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION being renamed to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF is not accurate, as device-private entries rely on migration support and the rename does not change the set of selecting architectures or gating semantics.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
> When a swap entry is installed in a page table, the mm must be added
> to init_mm.mmlist so that swapoff can find and unuse its swap entries.
> This double-checked locking pattern is currently open-coded in
> try_to_unmap_one() and copy_nonpresent_pte().
> 
> Move it into mm_prepare_for_swap_entries() in mm/internal.h and convert
> both callers so it can be reused by upcoming PMD-level swap entry code
> paths that also need to register the mm with swapoff.
> 
> copy_nonpresent_pte() previously inserted into &src_mm->mmlist rather
> than &init_mm.mmlist, but the insertion point is irrelevant, mmlist
> is a circular list and swapoff walks it entirely from init_mm.mmlist,
> so only membership matters, not position.
> 
> Reviewed-by: Dev Jain <dev.jain@arm.com>
> Reviewed-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
> Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> ---
>  mm/internal.h | 13 +++++++++++++
>  mm/memory.c   |  9 +--------
>  mm/rmap.c     |  7 +------
>  3 files changed, 15 insertions(+), 14 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/internal.h b/mm/internal.h
> index 8e0df3d1c6f9..0e3db9451e5c 100644
> --- a/mm/internal.h
> +++ b/mm/internal.h
> @@ -1956,4 +1956,17 @@ static inline int get_sysctl_max_map_count(void)
>  bool may_expand_vm(struct mm_struct *mm, const vma_flags_t *vma_flags,
>  		   unsigned long npages);
>  
> +/*
> + * Ensure @mm is on the init_mm.mmlist so swapoff can find it.
> + */

Comment fits into a single line and I don't think we need it (function name is
self-explaining enough).

> +static inline void mm_prepare_for_swap_entries(struct mm_struct *mm)
> +{
> +	if (list_empty(&mm->mmlist)) {
> +		spin_lock(&mmlist_lock);
> +		if (list_empty(&mm->mmlist))
> +			list_add(&mm->mmlist, &init_mm.mmlist);
> +		spin_unlock(&mmlist_lock);
> +	}
> +}
> +

Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

-- 
Cheers,

David

---

On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
> migrate_vma_collect_pmd() calls softleaf_to_folio() on a non-present
> PMD before checking the entry's type.  softleaf_to_folio() converts
> the entry's offset to a PFN, which is only meaningful for migration
> or device-private entries.
> 
> A PMD swap entry's offset is a swap offset, not a PFN, so the
> lookup would either return a bogus folio pointer or trip pfn_to_page
> validation on a debug kernel.  In the non-device-private path the
> returned folio is then unused (the OR short-circuits to
> migrate_vma_collect_skip()), but the lookup itself is already
> unsafe.
> 
> Move the softleaf_to_folio() call inside the device-private branch
> where the folio is actually needed, mirroring the equivalent
> change_non_present_huge_pmd() fix.
> 
> Reviewed-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
> Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
> ---
>  mm/migrate_device.c | 11 +++++++----
>  1 file changed, 7 insertions(+), 4 deletions(-)
> 
> diff --git a/mm/migrate_device.c b/mm/migrate_device.c
> index aa948db49501..36287e958b2a 100644
> --- a/mm/migrate_device.c
> +++ b/mm/migrate_device.c
> @@ -166,11 +166,14 @@ static int migrate_vma_collect_huge_pmd(pmd_t *pmdp, unsigned long start,
>  	} else if (!pmd_present(*pmdp)) {
>  		const softleaf_t entry = softleaf_from_pmd(*pmdp);
>  
> -		folio = softleaf_to_folio(entry);
> -
>  		if (!softleaf_is_device_private(entry) ||
> -			!(migrate->flags & MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_DEVICE_PRIVATE) ||
> -			(folio->pgmap->owner != migrate->pgmap_owner)) {
> +		    !(migrate->flags & MIGRATE_VMA_SELECT_DEVICE_PRIVATE)) {
> +			spin_unlock(ptl);
> +			return migrate_vma_collect_skip(start, end, walk);
> +		}
> +
> +		folio = softleaf_to_folio(entry);
> +		if (folio->pgmap->owner != migrate->pgmap_owner) {
>  			spin_unlock(ptl);
>  			return migrate_vma_collect_skip(start, end, walk);
>  		}

LGTM

Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>

-- 
Cheers,

David

---

On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
> CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION started life gating just PMD-level
> migration entries, but has grown to gate the entire PMD-level softleaf
> machinery: migration entries, device-private entries, and soon swap
> entries.

device-private really relies on migration support.

See do_huge_pmd_device_private() where we migrate back to CPU memory.

The whole concept is pased on migration. So that's why it currently makes sense.

So "has grown to" is not quite accurate. It's a different story with swap
entries (that can exist even without migration support).


> Rename CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD
> _SOFTLEAF to make this clear.  This is a pure rename: the set of
> selecting architectures (x86, arm64, s390, riscv, loongarch, and
> powerpc on PPC_BOOK3S_64) and the gating semantics are unchanged.

Hm, that makes me wonder what it would take to make this all just be implicitly
supported by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE.

Looks like someone would have the courage to touch stuff like arc, mips and
sparc64 (+arm and x86, but that's less of a concern :) ).

So yeah, renaming the config option makes sense. The config space is the wild west.

We have

	PGTABLE_HAS_HUGE_LEAVES

and stuff like

	HAVE_ARCH_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE_PUD
	HAVE_ARCH_SOFT_DIRTY
	ARCH_HAS_GIGANTIC_PAGE
	ARCH_HAS_PTE_SPECIAL

We do have a limited number of "SUPPORTS" (which is longer than HAS), but I
would either use


	ARCH_HAS_PMD_SOFTLEAVES

or

	HAVE_ARCH_PMD_SOFTLEAVES


Apart from that LGTM.

-- 
Cheers,

David

---

On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
> This is the preparatory part of the PMD page table swapin work.  The
> full PMD swap entry series has been split into two parts:
> 
>   1. this preparatory series, which contains the first 6 patches. Zi [1]
>      and Lance [2] suggested to separate this out from the core series.
>   2. the PMD swap entry core series, which depends on this one. I will
>      send this once the preparatory series is merged in mm-new as v3
>      as the combined is currently at v2 [1].
>      I have not marked this prep series as v3, as its not really adding
>      support for PMD swap entries.
> 
> This series does not introduce PMD swap entries and does not install any
> new page-table entry type.  It only cleans up existing PMD softleaf
> helpers and call sites so the follow-up PMD swap entry series can be
> smaller and easier to review.
> 
> It should be safe to merge independently.  

Agreed, thanks for working on this and splitting this part out!

-- 
Cheers,

David

---

On 7/1/26 22:39, Zi Yan wrote:
> On 1 Jul 2026, at 16:03, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
> 
>> On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
>>> CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION started life gating just PMD-level
>>> migration entries, but has grown to gate the entire PMD-level softleaf
>>> machinery: migration entries, device-private entries, and soon swap
>>> entries.
>>
>> device-private really relies on migration support.
>>
>> See do_huge_pmd_device_private() where we migrate back to CPU memory.
>>
>> The whole concept is pased on migration. So that's why it currently makes sense.
>>
>> So "has grown to" is not quite accurate. It's a different story with swap
>> entries (that can exist even without migration support).
>>
>>
>>> Rename CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD
>>> _SOFTLEAF to make this clear.  This is a pure rename: the set of
>>> selecting architectures (x86, arm64, s390, riscv, loongarch, and
>>> powerpc on PPC_BOOK3S_64) and the gating semantics are unchanged.
>>
>> Hm, that makes me wonder what it would take to make this all just be implicitly
>> supported by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE.
>>
>> Looks like someone would have the courage to touch stuff like arc, mips and
>> sparc64 (+arm and x86, but that's less of a concern :) ).
> 
> You mean me back in 2018[1]? ;)
> 
> At least I got a no go for s390.

hah! and 8 years later it seems to exist upstream :)

commit 10dd5a0009898ba35eafeb6087e5c83b84742ff1
Author: Gerald Schaefer <gerald.schaefer@linux.ibm.com>
Date:   Tue Jul 29 13:16:19 2025 +0200

    s390/mm: Enable THP_SWAP and THP_MIGRATION

    After hugetlbfs PTE_MARKER support for s390 introduced region-third and
    segment table swap entries, it is now possible to also enable THP_SWAP
    and THP_MIGRATION for s390.

So, with that out of the way, could you revive your patchset?


-- 
Cheers,

David

---

On 7/2/26 19:08, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 05:57:34PM +0100, Mark Brown wrote:
>> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 05:53:48PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
>>
>>
>>> Well we're very grateful for what coverage it has :) and that's nice to have it
>>> work with email like that also, makes life simpler.
>>
>>> Mark - are you running tests based on mm-new? I'm not sure where things stand on
>>> that?
>>
>> I currently test -next, mainline and stable plus my own stuff.
> 
> Ack thanks!
> 
> I guess in the long run then we can figure out a way to not necessarily even
> require mm-new at all, and instead let our CI try to handle as much as it can
> before -> linux-next :)
> 
> As that'd be the nicest way and help reduce confusion about branches for
> mm-next.

If we go the for-test route, we'd still have a branch where people/bots/tools
can do more elaborate tests when throwing stuff together. I still consider that
reasonable, but we'll have to see if we really need that.

-- 
Cheers,

David

review_comments.author: Zi Yan
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Zi Yan noted that device-private entries rely on migration support and suggested revisiting the rename of CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD_SOFTLEAF, as it may be possible to make PMD softleaf implicitly supported by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE. The reviewer also mentioned that someone would need to touch architectures like arc, mips, and sparc64 (+arm and x86) to enable implicit support.
review_comments.raw_body:
On 1 Jul 2026, at 16:03, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:

> On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
>> CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION started life gating just PMD-level
>> migration entries, but has grown to gate the entire PMD-level softleaf
>> machinery: migration entries, device-private entries, and soon swap
>> entries.
>
> device-private really relies on migration support.
>
> See do_huge_pmd_device_private() where we migrate back to CPU memory.
>
> The whole concept is pased on migration. So that's why it currently makes sense.
>
> So "has grown to" is not quite accurate. It's a different story with swap
> entries (that can exist even without migration support).
>
>
>> Rename CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD
>> _SOFTLEAF to make this clear.  This is a pure rename: the set of
>> selecting architectures (x86, arm64, s390, riscv, loongarch, and
>> powerpc on PPC_BOOK3S_64) and the gating semantics are unchanged.
>
> Hm, that makes me wonder what it would take to make this all just be implicitly
> supported by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE.
>
> Looks like someone would have the courage to touch stuff like arc, mips and
> sparc64 (+arm and x86, but that's less of a concern :) ).

You mean me back in 2018[1]? ;)

At least I got a no go for s390.


[1] https://lore.kernel.org/linux-mm/20180426142804.180152-1-zi.yan@sent.com/


Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

---

On 1 Jul 2026, at 16:50, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:

> On 7/1/26 22:39, Zi Yan wrote:
>> On 1 Jul 2026, at 16:03, David Hildenbrand (Arm) wrote:
>>
>>> On 6/30/26 18:34, Usama Arif wrote:
>>>> CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION started life gating just PMD-level
>>>> migration entries, but has grown to gate the entire PMD-level softleaf
>>>> machinery: migration entries, device-private entries, and soon swap
>>>> entries.
>>>
>>> device-private really relies on migration support.
>>>
>>> See do_huge_pmd_device_private() where we migrate back to CPU memory.
>>>
>>> The whole concept is pased on migration. So that's why it currently makes sense.
>>>
>>> So "has grown to" is not quite accurate. It's a different story with swap
>>> entries (that can exist even without migration support).
>>>
>>>
>>>> Rename CONFIG_ARCH_ENABLE_THP_MIGRATION to CONFIG_ARCH_SUPPORTS_PMD
>>>> _SOFTLEAF to make this clear.  This is a pure rename: the set of
>>>> selecting architectures (x86, arm64, s390, riscv, loongarch, and
>>>> powerpc on PPC_BOOK3S_64) and the gating semantics are unchanged.
>>>
>>> Hm, that makes me wonder what it would take to make this all just be implicitly
>>> supported by CONFIG_TRANSPARENT_HUGEPAGE.
>>>
>>> Looks like someone would have the courage to touch stuff like arc, mips and
>>> sparc64 (+arm and x86, but that's less of a concern :) ).
>>
>> You mean me back in 2018[1]? ;)
>>
>> At least I got a no go for s390.
>
> hah! and 8 years later it seems to exist upstream :)
>
> commit 10dd5a0009898ba35eafeb6087e5c83b84742ff1
> Author: Gerald Schaefer <gerald.schaefer@linux.ibm.com>
> Date:   Tue Jul 29 13:16:19 2025 +0200
>
>     s390/mm: Enable THP_SWAP and THP_MIGRATION
>
>     After hugetlbfs PTE_MARKER support for s390 introduced region-third and
>     segment table swap entries, it is now possible to also enable THP_SWAP
>     and THP_MIGRATION for s390.
>
> So, with that out of the way, could you revive your patchset?
>
Sure. Will put that on my TODO list.

Best Regards,
Yan, Zi

review_comments.author: Mike Rapoport
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Mike Rapoport questioned the purpose of mm-new branch and its relation to CI testing, pointing out that CI grabs patches from patchwork regardless of their presence in mm-new or not.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 11:48:24AM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> +cc Mike re: CI.
> 
> On Wed, Jul 01, 2026 at 04:46:13PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> > On Wed, 1 Jul 2026 09:04:33 +0100 Lorenzo Stoakes <ljs@kernel.org> wrote:
> >
> > > On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 12:50:24PM -0700, Andrew Morton wrote:
> > > > On Tue, 30 Jun 2026 09:34:37 -0700 Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev> wrote:
> > > >
> > > > > This is the preparatory part of the PMD page table swapin work.
> > > >
> > > > Thanks.
> > > >
> > > > This comes reasonably reviewed, so I'll queue it in mm-new.
> > > >
> > > > Why?
> > > >
> > > > - Human review coverage and AI review make me believe this isn't the
> > > >   final version at all, but I do believe this is something we want in
> > > >   7.2, and getting it under test early will help to push it along.
> > > >
> > > > - Getting it in there early means that others will base their work on
> > > >   material which will probably be upstreamed this cycle, so this gives
> > > >   them a more accurate base against which to work.
> > > >
> > > > Sashiko did find a lot to talk about, some of it pre-existing so can
> > > > people who work on this code please take a look at
> > > >
> > > > 	https://sashiko.dev/#/patchset/20260630164143.1595669-1-usama.arif@linux.dev
> > > >
> > > >
> > >
> > > Hm, my understanding was that mm-new is where we take everything submitted for
> > > early testing?
> > >
> > > Did something change?
> >
> > mm-new has always been a few day front-end to mm-unstable.  To protect
> > linux-next from brand new material.  It serves no other purpose.
> 
> Well it only serves a purpose if it gives us better testing.
> 
> We have a CI running tests now though, Mike - is that predicated on material
> being in mm-new or does it just grab the email?

CI grabs patches from patchwork and tries to apply them to one of the three
bases: mm-unstable, mm-new or the latest Linus' commit merged into mm.git.

If patches apply, the other bases are not checked. 

This patches applied cleanly to mm-unstable and successfully passed (at
least once :) )
https://github.com/linux-mm/linux-mm/actions/runs/28544575927/
 
> If the CI is just grabbing email I wonder whether there's much purpose in the
> mm-new branch at all.

I can't say that CI I run has a great coverage. I don't know what coverage
mm-new has though.
  
> Thanks, Lorenzo

-- 
Sincerely yours,
Mike.

review_comments.author: Mark Brown
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Mark Brown asked about the testing coverage of the mm-new branch, specifically whether he is running tests based on it.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 05:53:48PM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:
> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 04:08:03PM +0300, Mike Rapoport wrote:

> > I can't say that CI I run has a great coverage. I don't know what coverage
> > mm-new has though

> Well we're very grateful for what coverage it has :) and that's nice to have it
> work with email like that also, makes life simpler.

> Mark - are you running tests based on mm-new? I'm not sure where things stand on
> that?

I currently test -next, mainline and stable plus my own stuff.

review_comments.author: Lance Yang
review_comments.summary: Reviewer Lance Yang approved the patch, stating that adding softleaf_to_pmd() as a PMD counterpart to softleaf_to_pte() completes the symmetry of the softleaf abstraction for page table leaf entries and keeps feature patches focused on new functionality.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Tue, Jun 30, 2026 at 09:34:38AM -0700, Usama Arif wrote:
>Add softleaf_to_pmd() as the PMD counterpart to softleaf_to_pte(),
>completing the symmetry of the softleaf abstraction for page table
>leaf entries.
>
>The upcoming PMD swap entry support needs to construct PMD entries
>from swap entries. Converting existing swp_entry_to_pmd() callers
>to softleaf_to_pmd() in a prep patch keeps the feature patches
>focused on new functionality rather than mixing refactoring with
>new code.
>
>Acked-by: David Hildenbrand (Arm) <david@kernel.org>
>Reviewed-by: Zi Yan <ziy@nvidia.com>
>Signed-off-by: Usama Arif <usama.arif@linux.dev>
>---

LGTM. Feel free to add:

Reviewed-by: Lance Yang <lance.yang@linux.dev>

review_comments.author: SJ Park
review_comments.summary: Reviewer SJ Park questioned the purpose of mm-new, citing that it only serves a purpose if it provides better testing, and noted that CI is already running tests on patches from patchwork, regardless of whether they are in mm-new or not. They also expressed interest in contributing to the mm-ci repository and suggested adding DAMON kunit tests to the test set.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, 2 Jul 2026 16:08:03 +0300 Mike Rapoport <rppt@kernel.org> wrote:

> On Thu, Jul 02, 2026 at 11:48:24AM +0100, Lorenzo Stoakes wrote:

> > > > >   final version at all, but I do believe this is something we want in
> > > > >   7.2, and getting it under test early will help to push it along.
> > > > >
> > > > > - Getting it in there early means that others will base their work on
> > > > >   material which will probably be upstreamed this cycle, so this gives

> > > > >

> > > mm-new has always been a few day front-end to mm-unstable.  To protect

> CI grabs patches from patchwork and tries to apply them to one of the three

Awesome!

I found the CI script (mm-ci) on the GitHub organization.  Is the repo open to
contributions?  If so, between GitHub PR and patches to linux-mm@ what is more
preferred?  Seems it is not running DAMON specific tests, and I want to add
DAMON kunit test to the test set as a first step (DAMO selftest is bit flaky on
some systems), if people don't mind.

I or someone else may also want to add more tests like Cc list checking [1] in
future.

[1] https://lore.kernel.org/20260703190421.100480-1-sj@kernel.org


Thanks,
SJ
